name: Pomona
description: Pomona is the Roman goddess of fruit trees, gardens, and orchards. Unlike many other Roman deities, Pomona does not have a Greek counterpart. She is a uniquely Roman figure, deeply connected to the cultivation and nurturing of fruitful abundance. Pomona's name derives from the Latin word 'pomum,' meaning fruit, emphasizing her direct association with the bounty of the harvest. She is often depicted as a beautiful woman carrying a pruning knife or holding a basket overflowing with fruits. Pomona was revered for her dedication to her orchards and gardens, preferring their company to that of potential suitors. She possessed the power to protect and foster the growth of fruit trees, ensuring a plentiful harvest for the people. Her devotees sought her favor for the success of their orchards and vineyards, offering sacrifices of fruits and flowers. She spurned the advances of woodland gods and satyrs, ultimately finding love with Vertumnus, the god of seasons, gardens, and plant growth, who won her over through disguise and persistence. Their union symbolized the harmonious relationship between the seasons and the flourishing of the land. Pomona's worship highlights the Roman emphasis on agriculture and the importance of nurturing the natural world to sustain life.
subtitle: Goddess of Fruit Trees and Orchards

keyMyths.title: Pomona and Vertumnus
keyMyths.description: Vertumnus, god of seasons, desired Pomona. He disguised himself repeatedly to gain her trust, finally revealing his true form and winning her heart.

keyMyths.title: Pomona's Devotion
keyMyths.description: Pomona was so dedicated to her orchards that she shunned all suitors, preferring the company of her trees and the fruits they bore.

keyMyths.title: The Bountiful Harvest
keyMyths.description: Farmers prayed to Pomona for a plentiful harvest, offering her fruits and flowers to ensure the health and abundance of their orchards.

sources.author: Ovid
